questions.question_text: Which of the four government powers over real property is exercised when a city enforces a zoning ordinance?
questions.correct_answer: Police power
distractors: Eminent domain
Taxation
Escheat
questions.explanation: Police power is the government's authority to regulate private property use for the health, safety, morals, and general welfare of the public. Zoning ordinances, building codes, and environmental regulations are all exercises of police power. Unlike eminent domain, police power generally requires no compensation to the property owner. The four government powers over real estate are remembered with the mnemonic PETE: Police power, Eminent domain, Taxation, and Escheat.

questions.question_text: A city condemns a vacant lot to build a new fire station. The owner objects but must sell. This is an example of:
questions.correct_answer: Eminent domain
distractors: Police power
Escheat
Taxation
questions.explanation: Eminent domain is the government's inherent right to take private property for public use, provided the owner receives just compensation (fair market value). The legal process of actually exercising this right is called condemnation. The Fifth Amendment to the U.S. Constitution requires 'just compensation.' Police power regulates use without taking ownership. Escheat transfers ownership of ownerless property. Taxation collects revenue without transferring ownership.

questions.question_text: A property owner's land is not taken but its value is significantly reduced because a new highway is built adjacent to the property, creating noise and reduced access. The owner may have a claim for:
questions.correct_answer: Inverse condemnation
distractors: Nothing, no remedy is available
A zoning variance to offset the diminished property value
A county tax abatement equal to the lost value
questions.explanation: Inverse condemnation (also called a regulatory taking) occurs when government action substantially diminishes a property's value without physically taking it. The property owner can sue the government for compensation. While the legal standards are complex, courts have recognized inverse condemnation claims when government projects deprive owners of most or all economic use of their property. This doctrine protects owners from the government achieving a de facto taking without paying for it.

questions.question_text: Ad valorem property taxes are based on:
questions.correct_answer: The assessed value of the property
distractors: The original purchase price paid by the current owner
The replacement cost of all improvements on the property
The owner's gross annual income
questions.explanation: Ad valorem (Latin: 'according to value') means the tax is calculated as a percentage of the property's assessed value. The assessed value is determined by the local assessor, often as a percentage of market value (the assessment ratio). The tax rate (mill rate) is then applied to the assessed value. Property taxes are an exercise of the government's taxation power and are a specific lien against the property.

questions.question_text: Escheat is the government power by which:
questions.correct_answer: Property with no owner or heirs passes to the state
distractors: The government takes property for public use with compensation
The government regulates how private property may be used
The government places a tax lien for unpaid taxes
questions.explanation: Escheat is the government's right to acquire property when an owner dies intestate (without a will) and no heirs can be found, or when property is abandoned. The state steps in as the ultimate 'heir' to ensure property doesn't remain in legal limbo. This is the rarest of the four government powers (PETE). It is distinct from eminent domain (takes property with compensation for public use) and police power (regulates use without taking ownership).

questions.question_text: A property owner wants to operate a small hair salon from their home in a residentially-zoned neighborhood. The zoning ordinance prohibits commercial activity. The owner applies for permission to operate as a limited exception. This application is for a:
questions.correct_answer: A variance or special use permit from the zoning board
distractors: A condemnation proceeding filed with the county court
A nonconforming use license from the building department
A deed restriction amendment recorded by the HOA
questions.explanation: When a property owner wants to use land in a way that doesn't conform to current zoning, they can request a variance (a deviation from zoning standards due to hardship or unique property conditions) or a special use/conditional use permit (a use permitted by the zoning code only with board approval under specified conditions). A home-based business is typically handled through a special use permit. A nonconforming use is an existing use that predates a zoning change and is allowed to continue.

questions.question_text: A gas station has operated on a corner lot for 30 years. The city rezones the area as residential. The gas station is allowed to continue operating. This is an example of:
questions.correct_answer: A nonconforming use
distractors: A variance granted by the zoning board for a specific hardship
A special use permit for commercial operations in a residential zone
An easement by prescription through long-term continuous use
questions.explanation: A nonconforming use (also called a legal nonconforming use or 'grandfathered use') is a use that legally existed before the current zoning ordinance was enacted. It is allowed to continue even though it no longer conforms to current zoning requirements. However, nonconforming uses are typically not allowed to expand, and if the use is discontinued for a specified period, the owner may lose the nonconforming use rights.

questions.question_text: A utility company has the legal right to run power lines across the back of several private properties. This is BEST described as:
questions.correct_answer: An easement in gross
distractors: An appurtenant easement
A license
An encroachment
questions.explanation: An easement in gross benefits an individual or entity (the utility company) rather than a neighboring parcel of land. It does not require a dominant estate. Utility easements (power lines, pipelines, telecommunications) are classic examples of easements in gross. An appurtenant easement benefits a neighboring property (the dominant estate) and is transferred with that property when it is sold. A license is revocable permission. An encroachment is unauthorized — utility companies have legal authority through their easement.

questions.question_text: An easement appurtenant differs from an easement in gross in that an easement appurtenant:
questions.correct_answer: Benefits a neighboring parcel and transfers with that land
distractors: Belongs to an individual rather than a neighboring parcel
Requires a written agreement by both parties to be valid
Can only be created by court order, not by deed
questions.explanation: An easement appurtenant involves two parcels: the dominant estate (which benefits from the easement) and the servient estate (which is burdened by it). The easement 'runs with the land' — it automatically transfers when the dominant estate is sold, without needing to be mentioned in the new deed. A classic example: Lot A has no road access except across Lot B. Lot B has an easement appurtenant in favor of Lot A. When Lot A is sold, the new owner automatically gets the easement right.

questions.question_text: A homeowner gives a neighbor verbal permission to park in their driveway while the neighbor's driveway is being resurfaced. This permission is BEST described as:
questions.correct_answer: A license
distractors: An easement by prescription
An easement appurtenant
A lease
questions.explanation: A license is a personal, revocable permission to use someone else's land. It is not a property right and cannot be transferred. The homeowner can revoke it at any time without liability. Because it is not an interest in real property, a license does not need to be in writing (no Statute of Frauds requirement). If the neighbor began using the parking space under a claim of right, openly and continuously for the statutory period, they might eventually acquire an easement by prescription — but a simple verbal permission prevents this because the use is permissive, not hostile.

questions.question_text: After closing on a home, the buyer discovers that a neighbor's storage shed extends 2 feet onto the newly purchased property. This is called:
questions.correct_answer: An encroachment
distractors: An easement by necessity
A license
A deed restriction
questions.explanation: An encroachment is the unauthorized intrusion of a structure, improvement, or object from one property onto another's property without permission. Common examples include fences, sheds, driveways, and building overhangs. Encroachments are different from easements (which are authorized rights to use another's land). They can cloud title, create disputes, and complicate real estate sales. This is one reason why surveys are important — they identify encroachments that title searches miss.

questions.question_text: A homeowners association (HOA) prohibits painting houses any color other than white, beige, or gray. A new owner wants to paint their house blue. The HOA restriction is BEST characterized as:
questions.correct_answer: A private deed restriction (CC&R) that limits property use
distractors: A zoning ordinance imposed by the local government
An easement that restricts the use of adjacent properties
A taking that requires just compensation to the owner
questions.explanation: Covenants, Conditions, and Restrictions (CC&Rs) are private deed restrictions created by developers or HOAs that run with the land and bind all current and future owners. They are enforced by the HOA or neighboring property owners, not the government. This distinguishes them from zoning ordinances (government-imposed police power). HOA restrictions can be more limiting than zoning — for example, zoning may allow a home business, but CC&Rs may prohibit it. Violating CC&Rs can result in fines and legal action.

questions.question_text: A city enacts a new building code requiring all homes to have smoke detectors in every bedroom. This regulation is an exercise of:
questions.correct_answer: Police power, which regulates property for public health and safety
distractors: Eminent domain, which takes private property for government purposes
Taxation, which levies charges based on the value of real property
Escheat, which transfers abandoned property to state ownership
questions.explanation: Building codes are a classic exercise of police power — the government's authority to regulate property use for the health, safety, and welfare of the public. Police power does not require compensation to the property owner because it regulates rather than takes. Smoke detector requirements protect occupant safety. Remember PETE: Police power, Eminent domain, Taxation, Escheat.

questions.question_text: Which government power does NOT typically require compensation to the property owner?
questions.correct_answer: Police power
distractors: Eminent domain
Taxation
Escheat
questions.explanation: Police power allows the government to regulate property use without compensating owners, because it restricts use rather than taking ownership. Zoning, building codes, and environmental regulations are all police power actions. Eminent domain does require just compensation under the Fifth Amendment. Taxation requires payment from the owner (not to them). Escheat does not involve compensation because the owner is deceased without heirs.

questions.question_text: A coastal city bans all new construction within 500 feet of the shoreline to protect the environment. Property owners in the restricted zone lose significant development value but receive no payment. They argue this is an unconstitutional taking. The city's BEST defense is:
questions.correct_answer: The restriction is a valid exercise of police power
distractors: The owners consented to the restriction when they purchased
Environmental restrictions are exempt from constitutional protections
The restriction only applies to future construction, not existing rights
questions.explanation: The government can regulate land use under police power without compensation, provided the regulation serves a legitimate public purpose and does not deprive the owner of all economically beneficial use. Environmental protection is a recognized public welfare goal. However, if the regulation goes too far and eliminates all viable use, courts may find a regulatory taking requiring compensation. This tension between police power and takings is a heavily tested exam topic.

questions.question_text: The four government powers over real property can be remembered by the mnemonic:
questions.correct_answer: PETE
distractors: DUST
MARIA
OLDCAR
questions.explanation: PETE stands for the four government powers over real property: Police power (regulation), Eminent domain (taking for public use), Taxation (revenue collection), and Escheat (reversion to state). DUST describes elements of value. MARIA tests for fixtures. OLDCAR describes fiduciary duties. These mnemonics are frequently tested and worth memorizing.

questions.question_text: The legal process by which the government exercises its power of eminent domain is called:
questions.correct_answer: Condemnation
distractors: Foreclosure
Escheat
Annexation
questions.explanation: Condemnation is the legal proceeding through which the government takes private property under eminent domain. The owner receives just compensation (fair market value) as required by the Fifth Amendment. Foreclosure is a lender's remedy for defaulted loans. Escheat is the state's acquisition of property from a deceased person without heirs. Annexation is the process of attaching personal property to real property.

questions.question_text: The Fifth Amendment to the U.S. Constitution limits the government's power of eminent domain by requiring:
questions.correct_answer: Just compensation and a public purpose for the taking
distractors: A unanimous vote by the governing body before any taking
The owner's written consent before condemnation can proceed
Replacement property of equal or greater value for the owner
questions.explanation: The Fifth Amendment states that private property shall not 'be taken for public use, without just compensation.' This imposes two requirements: the taking must be for a public use (or public purpose), and the owner must receive just compensation, typically fair market value. The government does not need the owner's consent — that is the point of eminent domain. There is no requirement for replacement property or a unanimous vote.

questions.question_text: A city uses eminent domain to take a row of homes and sell the land to a private developer for a shopping center. The homeowners argue this is not a valid public use. Based on the Supreme Court's ruling in Kelo v. City of New London, the MOST likely outcome is:
questions.correct_answer: May be upheld if it serves a broad public purpose
distractors: The taking is invalid because land goes to a private entity
The homeowners prevail because commercial use is never public use
The city must hold a public referendum before transferring the land
questions.explanation: In Kelo v. City of New London (2005), the Supreme Court ruled that economic development qualifies as a public use under the Fifth Amendment, even when the property is transferred to a private party. This controversial decision broadened the definition of 'public use' to include 'public purpose.' However, many states responded by passing laws restricting the use of eminent domain for private economic development.

questions.question_text: A homeowner fails to pay property taxes for several years. The government may place which type of lien on the property?
questions.correct_answer: A specific, involuntary lien with priority over most other liens
distractors: A general lien attaching to all of the owner's property
A voluntary lien the owner agreed to when purchasing the property
A subordinate lien ranking behind the first mortgage in priority
questions.explanation: Property tax liens are specific (attached to the particular property), involuntary (imposed without the owner's consent), and superior (they take priority over virtually all other liens, including mortgages). This super-priority status exists because the government relies on property tax revenue to fund essential services. If taxes remain unpaid, the government can eventually foreclose and sell the property at a tax sale.

questions.question_text: A local government levies a one-time charge against properties that benefit from a new sidewalk and sewer installation on their street. This charge is called a:
questions.correct_answer: A special assessment
distractors: An ad valorem tax
A transfer tax
An impact fee
questions.explanation: A special assessment is a tax levied against specific properties that directly benefit from a public improvement (sidewalks, sewers, street lights, curb construction). Unlike ad valorem taxes, which are based on property value and fund general government operations, special assessments are tied to specific improvements and charged only to benefiting properties. Special assessments create specific liens against the assessed properties.

questions.question_text: A property has an assessed value of $200,000 and the tax rate is 25 mills. What is the annual property tax?
questions.explanation: One mill equals $0.001 (one-tenth of a cent). To calculate: $200,000 × 0.025 (25 mills) = $5,000. Alternatively, divide the assessed value by 1,000 and multiply by the mill rate: $200,000 ÷ 1,000 = $200 × 25 = $5,000. Common mistake: confusing mills with percentages — 25 mills is 2.5%, not 25%.

questions.question_text: In property taxation, a 'mill rate' represents:
questions.correct_answer: One-tenth of one cent, or $1 per $1,000 of assessed value
distractors: One cent per dollar of assessed value, or $10 per $1,000
One percent of the property's fair market value each year
A flat fee charged equally to all properties in a jurisdiction
questions.explanation: A mill is one-thousandth of a dollar ($0.001). A mill rate of 1 mill means $1 of tax per $1,000 of assessed value. If a property's assessed value is $100,000 and the mill rate is 30 mills, the annual tax is $100,000 × 0.030 = $3,000. Mill rates are set by local taxing authorities and can vary significantly between jurisdictions.

questions.question_text: A property owner dies without a will and with no known heirs. After a statutory waiting period, the property will:
questions.correct_answer: Escheat to the state government
distractors: Be sold at public auction by the probate court
Transfer to the county government as a tax forfeiture
Remain in the deceased owner's name indefinitely
questions.explanation: Escheat is the government's power to take ownership of property when the owner dies intestate (without a will) and no heirs can be located. This prevents property from being ownerless. The state becomes the default owner of last resort. Escheat is one of the four government powers over real property (PETE). The property does not simply go to auction or remain in the deceased's name — it transfers to the state through the escheat process.

questions.question_text: Which government power serves as the 'ultimate heir' when property has no identifiable owner?
questions.correct_answer: Escheat
distractors: Eminent domain
Police power
Taxation
questions.explanation: Escheat gives the government the role of ultimate heir — ensuring all property has a legal owner. If a person dies without a will and without any heirs who can be identified or located, the property escheats (reverts) to the state. Eminent domain takes property for public use with compensation. Police power regulates use. Taxation collects revenue. Escheat is the least commonly exercised of the four government powers.

questions.question_text: Escheat differs from eminent domain in that escheat:
questions.correct_answer: Escheat claims ownerless property; eminent domain takes from living owners
distractors: Requires just compensation, while eminent domain does not
Is exercised federally, while eminent domain is only by states
Applies to personal property; eminent domain applies to real property
questions.explanation: The key distinction is the circumstance: escheat occurs when property has no identifiable owner (the owner died without a will and without heirs), while eminent domain involves the government taking property from a living owner for public use. Eminent domain requires just compensation; escheat does not because there is no owner to compensate. Both powers can be exercised by federal and state governments, and both apply to real property.

questions.question_text: A bank account has been dormant for many years with no owner activity or contact. Under escheat laws, the funds will likely:
questions.correct_answer: Be turned over to the state after the dormancy period expires
distractors: Remain in the bank's possession permanently as unclaimed assets
Be divided equally among the account holder's last known contacts
Transfer to the Federal Reserve as abandoned financial property
questions.explanation: Escheat applies to both real and personal property, including bank accounts, stocks, insurance proceeds, and other financial assets. Most states have unclaimed property laws that require holders (banks, companies) to turn over dormant assets to the state after a specified period (typically 3-5 years). The rightful owner or their heirs can still claim the property from the state. This is how escheat works in modern practice beyond just real estate.

questions.question_text: A zoning board grants one property in a residential area a commercial zoning classification while all surrounding properties remain residential. This practice is called:
questions.correct_answer: Spot zoning, which is generally considered illegal
distractors: A variance, which is a standard zoning board function
Buffer zoning, which transitions between different use zones
Downzoning, which reduces the permitted density of an area
questions.explanation: Spot zoning is the singling out of a specific parcel for treatment different from surrounding properties, without a reasonable basis related to the public welfare. It is generally considered illegal because it benefits one owner at the expense of surrounding owners and the community plan. A variance is a minor deviation from zoning requirements based on hardship. Buffer zones are transitional areas between different use zones. Downzoning reduces permitted use intensity.

questions.question_text: A city changes the zoning of an area from commercial to residential. An existing warehouse in the newly rezoned area is allowed to continue operating but cannot expand. If the warehouse is destroyed by fire, the owner will MOST likely:
questions.correct_answer: Be unable to rebuild because the nonconforming use was terminated
distractors: Be allowed to rebuild the same size as a grandfathered use
Be required to build a larger structure meeting current zoning
Receive just compensation for loss of the commercial zoning rights
questions.explanation: Nonconforming uses are generally allowed to continue but cannot expand. In most jurisdictions, if a nonconforming structure is destroyed beyond a certain percentage (often 50-75%), the owner loses the right to rebuild it as a nonconforming use. They must then comply with current zoning. This rule encourages the gradual elimination of nonconforming uses. The owner does not receive compensation because no government taking occurred — it was a natural disaster.

questions.question_text: The purpose of a buffer zone in zoning is to:
questions.correct_answer: Provide a transition area between incompatible land use zones
distractors: Prevent any development within a specified distance of a roadway
Reserve land for future government facilities and public buildings
Increase property tax revenue by creating mixed-use development areas
questions.explanation: Buffer zones are transitional areas placed between incompatible zoning districts — for example, between a heavy industrial zone and a residential neighborhood. The buffer zone might be zoned for parks, offices, or light commercial use. The purpose is to minimize the negative impact of more intensive uses on less intensive ones. This is a standard zoning tool that promotes compatible land use patterns.

questions.question_text: When a zoning ordinance and a deed restriction conflict, which typically takes precedence?
questions.correct_answer: The more restrictive of the two controls applies
distractors: Zoning always prevails because government law supersedes private agreements
The deed restriction always prevails because it was recorded first
Neither restriction applies when they conflict
questions.explanation: When zoning and deed restrictions conflict, the more restrictive rule applies. For example, if zoning allows up to 35-foot buildings but CC&Rs limit buildings to 25 feet, the 25-foot limit controls. The deed restriction does not override zoning and zoning does not override deed restrictions — whichever imposes the greater limitation is the one that applies. This is a frequently tested concept on real estate exams.

questions.question_text: Deed restrictions (CC&Rs) are enforced by:
questions.correct_answer: The HOA or neighboring property owners through civil legal action
distractors: The local zoning board through administrative hearings and fines
The county sheriff through criminal enforcement proceedings
The state real estate commission through license discipline
questions.explanation: CC&Rs are private agreements, not government regulations. They are enforced by the HOA or by other property owners who benefit from the restrictions — typically through civil lawsuits seeking injunctions or damages. The government does not enforce private deed restrictions. The zoning board enforces zoning ordinances. The sheriff enforces criminal law. The real estate commission regulates licensees.

questions.question_text: A developer records CC&Rs for a new subdivision requiring minimum lot sizes of one acre. Thirty years later, can a new buyer who purchases a lot in this subdivision build on a half-acre lot?
questions.correct_answer: No, CC&Rs run with the land and bind all future owners
distractors: Yes, CC&Rs expire automatically after 25 years
Yes, if the buyer did not personally sign the CC&R agreement
No, but only if the original developer is still enforcing them
questions.explanation: CC&Rs 'run with the land' — they bind all subsequent owners regardless of whether they signed the original agreement. Purchasing property subject to recorded CC&Rs means accepting those restrictions. CC&Rs do not automatically expire, though some may contain sunset clauses. They can be modified or terminated by a specified vote of property owners or by a court if circumstances have changed so substantially that enforcement would be inequitable.

questions.question_text: CC&Rs that restrict property based on the race or religion of the buyer are:
questions.correct_answer: Unenforceable, per Shelley v. Kraemer
distractors: Enforceable if recorded before the Fair Housing Act of 1968
Valid only if unanimously approved by all current property owners
Enforceable privately but not in transactions with licensed agents
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court ruled in Shelley v. Kraemer (1948) that racially restrictive covenants are unenforceable because government enforcement of such private agreements constitutes state action violating the Fourteenth Amendment's Equal Protection Clause. The Fair Housing Act of 1968 later prohibited discriminatory restrictions in housing. Even if discriminatory CC&Rs still exist in old deed records, they are completely void and unenforceable.

questions.question_text: A prescriptive easement is acquired through:
questions.correct_answer: Open, continuous, hostile use of another's land for the statutory period
distractors: A written agreement between the dominant and servient estate owners
A court order granting access to a landlocked parcel of property
Government condemnation of a portion of the servient estate for access
questions.explanation: A prescriptive easement is acquired similarly to adverse possession — through open, continuous, hostile (without permission), and notorious use of another's land for the statutory period (varies by state, commonly 10-20 years). Unlike adverse possession, a prescriptive easement grants a right to use, not ownership. A written agreement creates an express easement. A court-ordered access creates an easement by necessity. Government condemnation is eminent domain.

questions.question_text: Owner A sells the front portion of their property to Buyer B, leaving the back portion landlocked with no road access. Buyer B's back parcel has no other access route. An easement across A's retained land would be classified as:
questions.correct_answer: An easement by necessity, created by the land division
distractors: An easement by prescription through long-term unauthorized use
An easement in gross, benefiting Buyer B personally
A license, which is revocable permission to cross A's property
questions.explanation: An easement by necessity is created when a parcel is landlocked through the division of a previously unified tract. The law implies an easement to provide access because without it, the landlocked parcel would be useless. The easement must be reasonably necessary, not merely convenient. It is created automatically by operation of law — no written agreement is required. An easement by prescription requires hostile use over a statutory period, which is different.

questions.question_text: Which of the following would terminate an easement?
questions.correct_answer: The dominant and servient estates merge into one ownership
distractors: The servient estate owner sells to a new buyer
The dominant estate owner stops using the easement for a year
The servient estate owner builds a fence across the easement
questions.explanation: Merger terminates an easement — when one person acquires both the dominant and servient estates, the easement is extinguished because an owner cannot have an easement across their own land. Other termination methods include release (written agreement), abandonment (intent to abandon plus non-use, not mere non-use alone), prescription, and expiration. Selling the servient estate does not terminate the easement — it runs with the land. A fence across an easement is an obstruction, not a termination.

questions.question_text: The key difference between a license and an easement is that a license:
questions.correct_answer: Is revocable and does not create a property interest
distractors: Must be in writing under the Statute of Frauds
Transfers automatically to new owners when the property is sold
Can only be granted by a government agency, not privately
questions.explanation: A license is personal, revocable permission to use another's land — it is not an interest in real property. An easement is a nonpossessory property interest that generally runs with the land and cannot be revoked at will. A license does not need to be in writing (no Statute of Frauds requirement). Licenses do not transfer when property is sold. Either private owners or government agencies can grant licenses.

questions.question_text: A movie studio pays a homeowner $500 to use their driveway for one day of filming. The homeowner can cancel the arrangement at any time. This is an example of:
questions.correct_answer: A license
distractors: An easement in gross
A lease
A profit
questions.explanation: This is a license because it is temporary, revocable permission to use property. Payment does not convert a license into an easement — the key factors are the temporary nature and the owner's right to revoke. A lease would grant exclusive possession for a defined term. An easement in gross is a permanent (or long-term) property interest. A profit (profit à prendre) allows taking resources like minerals or timber.

questions.question_text: A concert venue sells tickets allowing attendees to enter and occupy seats. These tickets represent:
questions.correct_answer: Licenses, because the venue can revoke entry rights
distractors: Leasehold interests, because attendees occupy assigned spaces
Easements, because attendees have a right to enter the building
Fee simple interests for the duration of the event
questions.explanation: Concert tickets and similar admissions are licenses — personal, revocable permissions to enter and use property for a specific purpose and time. The venue retains the right to revoke admission for cause (disruptive behavior, safety concerns). A leasehold requires exclusive possession and a defined term. An easement is a permanent interest in land. Fee simple is ownership. This real-world example helps illustrate the practical scope of licenses.

questions.question_text: A property owner verbally allows a neighbor to store a boat on their vacant lot. Two years later, the property owner sells the lot. The new owner tells the neighbor to remove the boat. The neighbor claims a right to continue using the lot. The new owner's STRONGEST argument is:
questions.correct_answer: The verbal permission was a license that terminated at sale
distractors: The neighbor acquired a prescriptive easement through two years of use
The verbal agreement became a binding lease after one year
The neighbor has an implied easement from the prior owner's permission
questions.explanation: A verbal permission to use land is a license — it is personal, revocable, and does not transfer when property is sold. The new owner has no obligation to honor the prior owner's license. Two years of permissive use does not create a prescriptive easement because the use was not hostile. A license cannot transform into a lease without the essential lease elements. An implied easement requires a pre-existing use at the time of a land division.

questions.question_text: A homeowner builds a new deck that extends 3 feet beyond the property line onto the neighbor's land. The neighbor can seek which of the following remedies?
questions.correct_answer: A court order for removal or a compensation agreement
distractors: An automatic transfer of ownership of the encroached strip
A building department fine for code violations only
No legal remedy exists for encroachments in most states
questions.explanation: The encroached-upon neighbor can seek a court injunction requiring removal of the encroaching structure, sue for monetary damages, or negotiate an easement or sale of the encroached strip. Encroachments do not automatically transfer ownership of the affected land. Building code violations are separate from property boundary disputes. Encroachments are not automatically legalized by completion of construction.

questions.question_text: Which professional is MOST qualified to determine whether an encroachment exists on a property?
questions.correct_answer: A licensed surveyor
distractors: A real estate appraiser
A home inspector
A title insurance agent
questions.explanation: A licensed surveyor physically measures and maps property boundaries, locating improvements and identifying any structures that cross boundary lines. Appraisers estimate value but do not measure boundaries. Home inspectors evaluate the condition of structures but do not determine property lines. Title insurance agents review recorded documents for ownership issues but encroachments are physical problems not visible in public records.

questions.question_text: A title insurance company issues a standard policy on a property. After closing, the buyer discovers the neighbor's fence encroaches onto the insured property. Will the title insurance policy cover this encroachment?
questions.correct_answer: Likely not, standard policies exclude survey matters
distractors: Yes, all encroachments are covered under standard policies
Only if the encroachment predates the policy effective date
No, encroachments are never covered by any title policy
questions.explanation: Standard title insurance policies typically exclude survey-related issues, including encroachments, boundary line disputes, and easements not shown in public records. An extended (or ALTA) policy may cover some of these issues, especially if a current survey is provided. This is why lenders often require both a survey and title insurance. Title insurance does not cover physical hazards like fire or flood — those are covered by homeowner's insurance.

questions.question_text: An encroachment differs from an easement in that an encroachment is:
questions.correct_answer: An unauthorized intrusion, while an easement is a legal right
distractors: A government-granted right; an easement is a private agreement
Temporary and ends at sale; an easement is permanent
Recorded in public records; an easement exists only verbally
questions.explanation: The key distinction is authorization: an encroachment is unauthorized — the structure or improvement crosses the boundary without the neighbor's permission. An easement is a legally authorized right to use another's land. An encroachment can be cured through removal, negotiation, or eventually ripen into a prescriptive easement if left unchallenged for the statutory period. Both can be discovered by surveys.
